title: practical english usage - Part 33
content:

The  auxiliary do is used to form the questions and negatives of other verbs, as 1  auxiliary       verb Do  has three main uses.

26 do:   introduction For have in expressions like have a bath, have a drink, have a walk, ►    23.

It weighs 37 kilos, (     H's-37 kUes-keayy^ Be heavy is not usually used in measuring expressions.

She is the same height as her father.

The room is ten metres long.  What colour are his eyes?

I wish I was ten kilos lighter.  What size are your shoes?

She is nearly my age.      He is six feet tall.

I'm nearly thirty, (     1-hai^tearly-thirty.) Be is also used to talk about age, height, length, weight, size, shape and colour.

2 age,   height,     weight,     size   and   colour Section 2 Be,  have      and   do grammar •  27  do: auxiliary verb For do with be,  ►     19.   For weak pronunciations of do and does, ►    315.   For do in short answers, ►    308.

Anna thinks there's something wrong with James, and so do I.

'That meat smells funny.' 'Yes, it does, doesn't it?' She doesn't like dancing, but I do. (= ... but I like dancing.) common in affirmative clauses as well as questions and negatives.

In cases where an auxiliary is used instead of a whole verb phrase (►   279), do is 5 ellipsis At no time did he lose his self-control.

Do is used in some structures with inversion (verb before subject) (►   270).

I don't do much  sport now, but I did play football when I was younger.

She thinks I don't love her, but I do love her.

Do sit down. You do look nice today!

We   can use do in an affirmative clause for emphasis.

but                                                   not I can't play football. (     I don't can play football.) I don't do much  in the evenings.

I don't like football. (     I like not football.)  Don't go.

verb do), but not with other auxiliary verbs (►   217).

We   use do to make negative clauses with ordinary verbs (including the ordinary 2 negatives What do you do in the evenings?

The  auxiliary do can make questions with the ordinary verb do.

Can you play football? (     Do you can play foofball?) not   i Do you like football? (     L  ke you football?) verbs (►   216). Compare: We   use do to make questions with ordinary verbs, but not with other auxiliary 1  questions The  auxiliary verb do is followed by infinitives without to. It has several uses.

27 d o: auxiliary      verb    ________________ __ grammar •  29  do so/it/that not (     ... My wife never docs so.)                     -> I always eat peas with honey. My wife never does.

*1 rode a camel in Morocco.' 'I'd love to do that, (     .. ,-te-do-se/)' to-do-so?) not I haven't got time to get the tickets. Who's going to do it? (     .. .-Who^s-going mentioned before. In other cases we  prefer do it/that or do alone.

We use do so mainly to refer to the same action, with the same subject, that was She rode a camel: she had never done so/that before.

I promised to get the tickets, and I will do so/it as soon as possible.

Do it and do that can be used instead of do so.

2  do   so   and   do    it/that He told me to get out, and I did so as quietly as possible.

Eventually she divorced Joshua. It was a pity she had not done so earlier.

'Put the car away, please.' 'I've already done so.' complement. It is usually rather formal.

The  expression do so can be used to avoid repeating a verb and its object or 1  do   so 29 do   so/it/that       ______________ For auxiliary verbs used instead of complete verb phrases, ►    279.

have     not                        done 'Close the door.' *1    done.' (     .. .-I-have   ) Note that the auxiliary verb is stressed in this structure.

'You should be getting dressed.' '1 am (doing).' Progressive forms are possible, but not very common.

He smokes more than he used to (do).

He didn't pass his exam, but he could have (done) if he'd tried harder.

'He's supposed to have locked the safe.' 'He has (done).' (AmE  He has.

'Come and stay with us.' 7 may (do), if I have the time.' (AmE  I may, if...
